mission.title: alertmanager: Querying alert state (silence/inhibition) lags behind (by up to `group_interval`)
mission.description: Querying alert state (silence/inhibition) lags behind (by up to `group_interval`). Requested by 5+ users.

steps.title: Check current alertmanager deployment
steps.description:
Verify your alertmanager version and configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n alertmanager -l app.kubernetes.io/name=alertmanager
helm list -n alertmanager 2>/dev/null || echo "Not installed via Helm"
```
This feature requires a working alertmanager installation.

steps.title: Review alertmanager configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant alertmanager configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n alertmanager -l app.kubernetes.io/name=alertmanager
kubectl get configmap -n alertmanager -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=alertmanager
```
If a silence is created, or _if alerts come in that belong to an already existing (silenced) alert group_, it takes some time until the affected alerts are actually displayed as silenced in the UI, presumably because alerts are only marked as

steps.title: Apply the fix for Querying alert state (silence/inhibition) lags behind (by…
steps.description:
An idea I had is to do those checks upon the API call, with the following advantages:
- It would naturally be outside of the "production critical" path of ingesting alerts and sending out notifications. Number of concurrent API calls (and if need be their maximum duration) could be limited easily.
- If you ask for something specific (alerts for a particular receiver, alerts matching a name or label), the check only needs to be performed on the alerts about to be returned.

See the source issue for community-verified solutions.

steps.title: Verify the feature works
steps.description:
Test that the new capability is working as expected:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n alertmanager -l app.kubernetes.io/name=alertmanager
kubectl get events -n alertmanager --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm the feature described in "Querying alert state (silence/inhibition) lags behind (by…" is functioning correctly.

resolution.summary:
An idea I had is to do those checks upon the API call, with the following advantages:
- It would naturally be outside of the "production critical" path of ingesting alerts and sending out notifications. Number of concurrent API calls (and if need be their maximum duration) could be limited easily.

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with alertmanager installed or the issue environment reproducible.
